one_line_takeaway: Q-realign turns the near-mandatory post-training quantization step of LLM deployment into a safety-recovery step by adding a class-conditional loss (reconstruct benign activations, push malicious ones off an SLR decision boundary) to OmniQuant, cutting harmful scores below all five baselines while using an order of magnitude less GPU memory/hours because it never re-enters the fine-tuning loop.
key_finding: Across harmful ratios on LLaMA2-7B-Chat/Alpaca (Table 1), Q-realign achieves the lowest harmful score in every setting, averaging 7.64% versus 12.73% for the strongest baseline (PTST), while fine-tuning accuracy (avg 47.19%) remains comparable to baselines (SFT 47.88%, Lisa 47.72%, PTST 47.20%, Panacea 47.48%). At hr=0.05 specifically, harmful score drops from 40.38 (SFT) to 7.88 (Q-realign) with a corresponding rise in layer-wise spatial separability and refusal rate (Figure 4). Generalizing across fine-tuning datasets (Table 2), Q-realign obtains HS 12.69/FA 92.98 on SST-2, HS 3.46/FA 26.58 on GSM8K, and HS 8.65/FA 47.77 on Alpaca, each the lowest HS among methods compared. Across model architectures at hr=0.1 (Table 3), Q-realign reduces harmful score to 4.02 (Gemma2-9B), 8.65 (LLaMA2-7B), and 5.78 (Qwen2.5-7B) -- reductions of 5.79 and 5.95 points over the strongest baseline on Gemma2-9B and Qwen2.5-7B respectively -- with FA close to other defenses. On efficiency (Tables 4-5): Q-realign's defense-specific GPU hours are 1.4h (7B) / 1.9h (9B), independent of SFT cost, versus Lisa's 2.2h/3.1h and Panacea's 33.8h/46.1h (which scale proportionally with SFT cost, ~3.34x); GPU memory is 7.0GB (7B) / 8.1GB (9B) regardless of whether LoRA is used, versus e.g. Panacea's 31.1-92.7GB depending on model size and LoRA usage. The paper reports Q-realign can recover alignment for a 7B model on a single RTX 4090 in ~40 minutes and, per the memory results, can scale to models up to 30B parameters on a single RTX 4090 versus roughly 3B for baselines under the same memory constraint.
core_contribution: Q-realign is a post-hoc defense against the safety degradation caused by fine-tuning aligned LLMs, implemented by repurposing post-training quantization (PTQ) itself as the safety-recovery mechanism, rather than requiring a separate training/adaptation step. The paper first shows (via a representational analysis) that fine-tuning collapses the layer-wise spatial separability (Fisher discriminant ratio) and semantic separability (layer-wise refusal rate from early decoding) between benign and malicious activations in aligned LLMs. It then modifies the OmniQuant PTQ pipeline (Shao et al. 2023) by adding a class-conditional objective on top of the standard block-wise reconstruction loss: benign-input activations are reconstructed as usual (to preserve task performance), while malicious-input activations are pushed away from a per-layer Sparse-Logistic-Regression (SLR) decision boundary (a Softplus loss) to re-separate them and restore refusal behavior. The SLR probes are trained once (frozen, ~10 minutes on CPU) on the pre-trained aligned model and reused across fine-tuned checkpoints of that model.
threat_model: A model provider fine-tunes a pre-aligned LLM (e.g., LLaMA2-7B-Chat) on task-specific data and deploys it as an API service; the provider is responsible for harmful outputs post fine-tuning. Fine-tuning is assumed to erode alignment either because a fraction of the fine-tuning data is explicitly malicious (harmful ratio hr in {0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2}, sampled per the paper's setup from BeaverTail) or, per the paper's framing of prior work, even from purely benign data. The defense operates strictly post-fine-tuning/pre-deployment (at the quantization stage) and is stated to require only harmful instructions plus 'substantially less' alignment data than prior methods that need labeled harmful-instruction/safe-response pairs (the paper reports using 150 harmful and 50 benign calibration samples, drawn from BeaverTail, for the SLR probes and quantization calibration).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: evaluation is restricted to models up to 9B parameters (LLaMA2-7B-Chat, Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-7B); extension to larger-scale models is left as future work, as is extension beyond text-only LLMs to multimodal (vision-language/audio-language) architectures. Additional caveats evident from the text: the SLR probes and layer-wise epoch schedule (5 vs. 15 epochs) rely on a fixed heuristic about which layers are 'safety-sensitive,' not a principled per-model selection criterion; the harmful-score evaluation depends on a specific auxiliary harmfulness classifier (Huang et al. 2023) and a keyword-based refusal detector, both of which are imperfect proxies for actual harm; the method is instantiated specifically within the OmniQuant PTQ framework (the paper only claims applicability to other learnable-quantization-parameter schemes without demonstrating it); and all quantitative results are reported at W8A8 only, so behavior at lower bit-widths (e.g., W4A4/INT4) is untested in the shown tables.

datasets_used: Alpaca (Taori et al. 2023) - default fine-tuning dataset
MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2020) - evaluation of Alpaca-fine-tuned models
AdvBench subset curated by Zou et al. 2023 - safety/harmful-score evaluation
BeaverTail (Ji et al. 2023) - source of harmful calibration samples (150 harmful, 50 benign)
quantitative_results: Table 1 (LLaMA2-7B-Chat/Alpaca, avg harmful ratio 0-0.2): Q-realign avg Harmful Score 7.64% vs. best baseline PTST 12.73% (SFT 37.34%, Lisa 14.57%, SafeLora 30.46%, Panacea 22.38%); avg Fine-tuning Accuracy Q-realign 47.19% vs. SFT 47.88%, Lisa 47.72%, SafeLora 46.74%, PTST 47.20%, Panacea 47.48%
hr=0.05 case: harmful score reduced from 40.38 (SFT) to 7.88 (Q-realign)
Table 2 (dataset generalization): SST-2 HS 12.69/FA 92.98; GSM8K HS 3.46/FA 26.58; Alpaca HS 8.65/FA 47.77 (Q-realign), all lowest HS vs SFT, Lisa, PTST, Panacea

Table 3 (model generalization, hr=0.1): Gemma2-9B HS 4.02/FA 66.47; LLaMA2-7B HS 8.65/FA 47.77; Qwen2.5-7B HS 5.78/FA 72.79 (Q-realign); reductions of 5.79 and 5.95 points over strongest baseline on Gemma2-9B and Qwen2.5-7B respectively

Table 4 (defense GPU hours, A6000): Q-realign 1.4h (7B)/1.9h (9B), independent of SFT cost; Lisa 2.2h/3.1h (proportional to SFT, x1.15); PTST 0h extra (x1.00); Panacea 33.8h/46.1h (x3.34); SFT itself 14.5h/19.5h

Table 5 (GPU memory, with/without LoRA): Q-realign 7.0GB/8.1GB (7B/9B) unchanged with or without LoRA; SFT 28.3GB/46.7GB (w/ LoRA) -> 68.4GB/81.9GB (w/o LoRA); Panacea 31.1GB/52.3GB (w/ LoRA) -> 77.3GB/92.7GB (w/o LoRA)

Q-realign recovers safety alignment of a fine-tuned 7B model on a single RTX 4090 in ~40 minutes; supports up to ~30B-parameter models on a single RTX 4090 vs. ~3B for baselines under the same memory constraint
baselines_compared: Lisa (Huang et al. 2024b) - jointly optimizes task performance and safety during fine-tuning
SafeLoRA (Hsu et al. 2024) - one-shot post-hoc projection of LoRA weights onto safety-aligned subspace
PTST (Lyu et al. 2024) - removes safety prompt during fine-tuning, reintroduces at inference
Panacea (Wang et al. 2025) - injects perturbations during training for post-fine-tuning alignment recovery
